instructions: There are 10 questions in total, consisting of 8 one-mark and 2 two-mark questions.
A wrong answer will incur a penalty of 0.33 marks for each one-mark question and 0.66 marks for each two-mark question.

q.value: The Fourier Transform of $e^{-at}u(t)$, where $a>0$, is   1 Mark

explanation.value: Using the standard Fourier Transform pair, $e^{-at}u(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} \dfrac{1}{a+j\omega}$ for $a>0$.

q.value: The Fourier Transform of $\displaystyle \frac{\sin(at)}{t}$, where $a>0$, is $X(\omega)$. Using the duality property of the Fourier Transform, the Fourier Transform of $X(t)$ is   1 Mark

explanation.value: Using the Fourier Transform pair $\dfrac{\sin(at)}{t} \xleftrightarrow{FT} \pi\,\operatorname{rect}\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2a}\right)$, we have $x(t)=\dfrac{\sin(at)}{t}$ and $X(\omega)=\pi\,\operatorname{rect}\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2a}\right)$. By the duality property, $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} X(\omega) \Rightarrow X(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} 2\pi x(-\omega)$. Therefore, $\mathcal{F}\{X(t)\}=2\pi\dfrac{\sin(-a\omega)}{-\omega}=2\pi\dfrac{\sin(a\omega)}{\omega}$.

q.value: Suppose $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} X(\omega)$. The Fourier Transform of $t\,x(t)$ is   1 Mark

explanation.value: Using the differentiation property of Fourier Transform, $t\,x(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} j\dfrac{dX(\omega)}{d\omega}$.

q.value: If $x(t)=2\delta(t-1)-2\delta(t+1)$, then its Fourier Transform is   1 Mark

explanation.value: Using $\delta(t-t_0) \xleftrightarrow{FT} e^{-j\omega t_0}$, we get $X(\omega)=2e^{-j\omega}-2e^{j\omega}=2(e^{-j\omega}-e^{j\omega})$. Since $e^{-j\omega}-e^{j\omega}=-2j\sin(\omega)$, therefore $X(\omega)=-4j\sin(\omega)$.

q.value: If $x(t)$ is a real and even signal, then its Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$ is   1 Mark

options: Complex and neither even nor odd

explanation.value: A real even time-domain signal has a Fourier Transform that is purely real and even. This is a standard symmetry property frequently tested in GATE.

q.value: If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} X(\omega)$, then the Fourier Transform of $t\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ is   1 Mark

explanation.value: First, $\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} \xleftrightarrow{FT} j\omega X(\omega)$. Using the property $t\,x(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} j\dfrac{dX(\omega)}{d\omega}$, we obtain $t\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} \xleftrightarrow{FT} j\dfrac{d}{d\omega}[j\omega X(\omega)] = -\dfrac{d}{d\omega}[\omega X(\omega)]$.

q.value: If $x(t)=\cos(\omega_0t)$, then its Fourier Transform is   1 Mark

explanation.value: Using the standard Fourier Transform pair, $\cos(\omega_0t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} \pi\left[\delta(\omega-\omega_0)+\delta(\omega+\omega_0)\right]$.

q.value:
If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} X(\omega)$ and $h(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} H(\omega)$, then the Fourier Transform of $x(t)*h(t)$ is  
1 Mark

explanation.value: The convolution property states that convolution in the time domain corresponds to multiplication in the frequency domain: $x(t)*h(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} X(\omega)H(\omega)$.

q.value: A signal has Fourier Transform $$X(\omega)=\frac{4}{4+\omega^2}.$$ The value of $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x(t)\,dt$$ is   2 Mark

explanation.value: Using the Fourier Transform property $$X(0)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x(t)dt.$$ Here, $$X(0)=\frac{4}{4+0}=1.$$ Therefore, $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x(t)dt=1.$$

q.value: Let $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\dfrac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ and $x(t)=\operatorname{sinc}^2(t)$. The energy of $x(t)$ is       2 Mark

explanation.value: Using the Fourier Transform pair $\operatorname{sinc}(t) \xleftrightarrow{FT} \operatorname{rect}\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}\right)$ and the multiplication-convolution property, $x(t)=\operatorname{sinc}^2(t)$ has the Fourier Transform $X(\omega)=\Lambda\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}\right)$, where $\Lambda(u)=1-|u|$ for $|u|\leq1$ and $0$ otherwise. Thus, $X(\omega)=1-\dfrac{|\omega|}{2\pi}$ for $|\omega|\leq2\pi$ and $X(\omega)=0$ otherwise. Applying Parseval's theorem, $$E=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|X(\omega)|^2d\omega$$=$$\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-2\pi}^{2\pi}\left(1-\frac{|\omega|}{2\pi}\right)^2d\omega=\frac{2}{3}.$$
